project_description.text: The 1980 XIII Olympic Winter Games at Lake Placid, New York will include construction or upgrade of major venues such as a new Field House/Arena, a refrigerated 400-meter Speed Skating Oval at the high school, 70 and 90 Meter Ski Jumps at Intervale, refrigerated bobsled and luge runs at Mt. Van Hoevenberg, expanded Alpine facilities at Whiteface Mountain, an Olympic Village at Ray Brook (designed for after-use as a federal correctional institution), and a Television Center on the Chubb River. The program is supported by a roughly $50 million LPOOC construction budget plus about $9.3 million in state improvements, along with infrastructure upgrades to water, sewer, power, and communications systems.

affected_community.text: The directly affected communities are the Town of North Elba (including the Village of Lake Placid), the Village of Saranac Lake, and the Town of Wilmington, with secondary effects in surrounding Essex and Franklin County towns such as Keene, Jay, Harrietstown, Elizabethtown, and others. The area has a permanent population of roughly 6,500–12,000 that swells with seasonal residents and tourists, and faces challenges including high seasonal unemployment, incomes well below the state average, nearly 11% of families in poverty, and overcrowded or substandard housing in some towns. Specific neighbors include residents along School Street, Old Military Road, River Street, and Route 86, the Lake Placid Central School, the John Brown Farm National Historic Site, and recreational users of the Adirondack Forest Preserve.

alternatives_overview.text: The EIS evaluated a No Olympics alternative, alternative sites for each major facility, alternative distribution patterns concentrating or dispersing venues, and growth scenarios ranging from a no-Olympics baseline through low-, mid-, and high-range projections. Site alternatives examined included Bassett Mountain and Mt. Whitney for the 90 Meter Ski Jump; the Horse Show Grounds, Fish & Game Club, Holiday Inn, and existing Ray Brook complex for the Field House, Speed Skating Oval, and Olympic Village; and the Airport/Horse Show Grounds for the Television Center to avoid the Chubb River floodplain. Additional alternatives addressed transportation (peripheral parking and shuttle buses versus no plan, and possible reactivation of the Remsen–Lake Placid rail line), funding shortfalls, energy conservation and waste-heat recovery, and wilderness management approaches; the analysis concluded the LPOOC's proposed program generally has the least cumulative environmental impact.

environmental_impact.text: Construction will cause short-term soil disturbance, erosion, dust, noise, and tree clearing—including roughly 57 acres of forest at Ray Brook, about 10 acres at Whiteface, and filling of about 3,000 square feet of the Ausable River floodplain at the 90 Meter Ski Jump—with potential siltation of the Chubb, Ausable, and Ray Brook waterways. The 266-foot ski jump tower at Intervale will create a significant long-term visual impact on the rural setting of John Brown's Farm and on travel corridors along Routes 73 and 86, while the refrigerated venues, Olympic Village, and Television Center will commit land, energy, and water resources over the long term. Olympic-induced growth is projected to add thousands of seasonal residents, hundreds of vacation homes and lodging units, and increased traffic and emissions by 1990, straining landfill capacity, electrical supply, and infrastructure, though no significant long-term cumulative impact on the Adirondack Forest Preserve or High Peaks Wilderness viewshed is anticipated.

public_response.text: A North Elba town referendum approved the Olympic concept, and household surveys showed strong satisfaction with local police and fire services, but residents and officials voiced concerns about fiscal burdens, with the LPOOC affirming the Games will not proceed if they impose severe local costs. The 90 Meter ski jump's height drew particular controversy, with the Adirondack Park Agency granting conceptual approval at Intervale while NYSDEC Commissioner Peter A.A. Berle voted to disapprove, and the State Historic Preservation Officer found no adverse effect on John Brown's Farm. Extensive consultation occurred through Task Force meetings, three public information meetings, local goals sessions in five communities, and the LPOOC Environmental Council, with written comments from federal and state agencies and environmental groups including the Adirondack Council, Sierra Club, Wilderness Society, and Natural Resources Defense Council raising issues such as NEPA compliance at Whiteface, floodplain siting of the TV Center, and visual impacts on the High Peaks.

value.name: Field House/Arena – Horse Show Grounds
value.description: Locating the new Field House and refrigerated Speed Skating Oval on the North Elba Park District-owned Horse Show Grounds along Route 73, approximately 1¼ miles from Lake Placid Village center, rather than in the Village Center as proposed by LPOOC.

value.name: Field House/Arena – Fish and Game Club Site
value.description: Locating the Field House and Speed Skating Oval on the open, privately owned Fish and Game Club site on Old Military Road, approximately one mile southwest of Lake Placid Village center.

value.name: No Refrigeration of Speed Skating Oval
value.description: Retaining the existing natural-ice Speed Skating Oval without installing a permanent refrigeration system, avoiding displacement of the High School running track and baseball field but failing to meet IOC/International Skating Union requirements.

value.name: Press Center – Craig Wood Golf Course Shell Structure
value.description: Constructing a new shell structure of approximately 42,000 sq ft at the Craig Wood Golf Course for use as the Press Center during the Games, with potential indoor recreational after-use such as tennis.

value.name: Press Center – Other Existing Structures
value.description: Using other existing buildings (e.g., Hotel Marcy, large hotels, or institutional facilities such as Ray Brook or Will Rogers Hospital) as the Press Center on a temporary basis during the Games.

value.name: Olympics Administration – Other Existing Structures
value.description: Housing Olympic administrative functions in available existing building space (Hotel Marcy, lodging facilities, or institutional space) rather than in the Lake Placid Town Hall.

value.name: Television Center – Airport/Horse Show Grounds
value.description: Constructing a shell structure at the Lake Placid Airport/Horse Show Grounds site for use as the Television Center, with permanent after-use as an airport hangar, avoiding flood-plain impacts of the Old Military Road (Chubb River) site.

value.name: Television Center – Other Existing Structures
value.description: Using other existing structures (e.g., Hotel Marcy, Ray Brook, or Will Rogers Hospital) to accommodate the Television Center's approximately 60,000 sq ft floor-area requirement.

value.name: 90/70-Meter Ski Jump – Mt. Whitney
value.description: Siting the 90- and 70-Meter Ski Jumps on the northeast slope of Mt. Whitney in the Town of North Elba, requiring a tower of approximately 160–200 feet but presenting access limitations via an unimproved private road through State Forest Preserve land.

value.name: 90/70-Meter Ski Jump – Bassett Mountain
value.description: Siting the 90- and 70-Meter Ski Jumps on Bassett Mountain in the Town of Jay, which would require only a ~60-foot tower but would entail substantial new disturbance (~18 acres cleared, ~48,500 cu yds fill) in a Resource Management land-use area with no identified after-use entity.

value.name: 90-Meter Jump – Temporary/Demountable Tower at Intervale
value.description: Constructing a temporary or demountable in-run tower at the Intervale site to reduce permanent visual impact, though this would preclude the permanent training facility intent and is prohibited under the federal funding legislation.

value.name: Opening Ceremony – Arena/Speed Skating Oval Complex
value.description: Holding the Opening Ceremony at the Lake Placid Village Arena and Speed Skating Oval complex rather than at the Horse Show Grounds or Intervale, providing a symbolic urban focus but with limited crowd capacity of approximately 10,000–15,000.

value.name: Luge – Alternative Alignment Parallel to Bobsled Run
value.description: Using an alternative finish alignment at Mt. Van Hoevenberg without the 'Omega' curve, reducing soil and vegetation disturbance by approximately 0.5 acres and earth removal by ~20,500 cu yds compared to the LPOOC preferred alignment.

value.name: Luge – Combined Bobsled-Luge at Mt. Van Hoevenberg
value.description: Modifying the existing Mt. Van Hoevenberg Bobsled run to accommodate Luge sleds via sidings, avoiding separate construction, but rejected due to incompatible course requirements, scheduling conflicts, and safety hazards.

value.name: Luge – Upper Abandoned 1932 Bobsled Run
value.description: Using the upper half-mile of the original 1932 Bobsled alignment as part of a new Luge run, but this alignment is only ~804 m long, requires extended utility and access roads, and has unfavorable sun exposure increasing refrigeration costs.

value.name: Luge – Intervale Ski Jump Site
value.description: Siting the Luge run at the Intervale Jump Complex, which would require extensive cut and fill and a ~100-foot trestle above the hill crest to achieve gradient requirements, deemed impractical due to cost and aesthetics.

value.name: Olympic Village – Holiday Inn Site
value.description: Constructing the Olympic Village on the slope southwest of the Holiday Inn in Lake Placid Village, providing close proximity to Olympic venues but requiring disturbance of approximately 10–12 acres of sloped, wooded terrain with no suitability for correctional facility after-use.

value.name: Olympic Village – Old Military Road (Chubb River) Site
value.description: Locating the Olympic Village on the open Old Military Road site approximately one mile from Lake Placid Village center, but the site's irregular shape, flood-plain constraints, railroad tracks, and power lines make it unsuitable for permanent residential use.

value.name: Olympic Village – Horse Show Grounds
value.description: Locating the Olympic Village on the Horse Show Grounds approximately 1½ miles from Lake Placid Village center, offering a flat open site suitable for housing or lodging after-use but requiring sewer and water extensions and not adaptable to correctional facility use.

value.name: Olympic Village – Former Ray Brook Rehabilitation Center (Camp Adirondack) Site
value.description: Reusing or demolishing the existing Ray Brook Rehabilitation Center complex north of Ray Brook as the Olympic Village site, avoiding disturbance of the undeveloped south parcel but potentially displacing the existing state minimum-security facility (Camp Adirondack).

value.name: Olympic Village – No Correctional Facility After-Use
value.description: Building the Olympic Village without a Federal Bureau of Prisons correctional facility as the designated after-use, instead relying on alternative after-uses such as public/private lodging, housing, or a national winter sports training center.

value.name: Olympic Village – Locate Federal Correctional Facility Elsewhere in Northeast
value.description: Siting the Federal Youth Correctional Institution in another location in the Northeast closer to metropolitan areas to improve inmate family access, foregoing the Olympic Village interim use at Ray Brook.

value.name: Nordic Facilities – Adjacent Private Lands (Mt. Van Hoevenberg)
value.description: Expanding cross-country ski trails onto private lands adjoining the Mt. Van Hoevenberg Recreation Area to the east, eliminating the need to clear or operate mechanical equipment in the designated High Peaks Wilderness Area.

value.name: Nordic Facilities – Intensive Use Area Only (Mt. Van Hoevenberg)
value.description: Confining trail expansion entirely within the existing Intensive Use Area at Mt. Van Hoevenberg, but topography limits achievable trail length to less than 2.5 km, insufficient to meet FIS 25 km loop requirements.

value.name: Nordic Facilities – High Peaks Wilderness (South Meadow)
value.description: Routing the additional 10 km of cross-country trail through the South Meadow section of the High Peaks Wilderness Area, which offers suitable terrain and ties into existing trails, but requires an APSLMP amendment to permit motorized grooming equipment in a Wilderness Area.

value.name: Alpine – Cloudsplitter Trail (Whiteface Mountain)
value.description: Reopening the previously cut Cloudsplitter trail on the upper slopes of Whiteface Mountain as the additional FIS-approved Alpine trail, but the trail was closed due to severe westerly winds causing bare, icy conditions and lift-operation hazards.

value.name: Alpine – Separate Finish Areas for Downhill Courses (Whiteface)
value.description: Providing separate finish areas for the Men's and Women's Downhill courses rather than a combined finish, avoiding the stream crossover but requiring duplication of spectator stands, timing, and press facilities at increased cost.

value.name: Alpine – Pump House Crossover (Whiteface)
value.description: Placing the Women's Downhill crossover near the existing pump house where the stream is wider and terrain steeper, resulting in a larger structure, greater excavation, and worse stream siltation than the proposed mid-station crossover.

value.name: Alpine – No Thruway Lift (No Action for Lift)
value.description: Foregoing the proposed Thruway lift at Whiteface Mountain, requiring competitors and officials to access Slalom and Giant Slalom starts via the main lift and Lift #2 in a high-wind area, potentially limiting or denying access during the Olympics.

value.name: Alpine – No Snowmaking Expansion (No Action for Snowmaking)
value.description: Not expanding the snowmaking system at Whiteface Mountain, risking inability to hold Alpine events if natural snow is deficient during the 1980 Games and perpetuating unreliable recreational use and revenues.

value.name: Alpine – New Base Lodge at Alternate Location (Whiteface)
value.description: Constructing an entirely new base lodge across the river from the existing lodge rather than expanding the current structure, providing less skier interference from slopes but at higher cost and with greater environmental disturbance to the West Branch Ausable.

value.name: Alpine – No Base Lodge Expansion (No Action for Lodge)
value.description: Retaining the existing inadequate base lodge without expansion, requiring substantial investment in temporary facilities for press and officials during the Olympics and continuing to underserve peak recreational skier numbers.

value.name: Bobsled Run – No Refrigeration (No Action)
value.description: Not refrigerating the Mt. Van Hoevenberg Bobsled run, risking inability to hold bobsledding events in 1980 due to insufficient ice and forgoing the long-term benefit of an extended, reliable bobsled season.

subthemes: Urban Expansion and Land Use Change

themes.justification: The project involves major venue construction, an Olympic Village, and infrastructure upgrades driving land use change and built environment transformation (Urban Development), while transportation alternatives including shuttle systems and rail reactivation reflect mobility network concerns (Transportation Infrastructure). Public controversy over the ski jump's visual impact, floodplain siting, and fiscal burdens on a low-income community illustrates place-based development conflict and community resistance.

public_commenters.name: Community Services Administration
public_commenters.quote: we are concerned that the unemployed and economically-disadvantaged residents of the community be included in the economic benefits expected to be generated by the Olympic Program

public_commenters.name: Department of Agriculture Forest Service
public_commenters.quote: The traffic emission of carbon monoxide (CO) receives more emphasis than emission of nitrogen oxides (NO,) and hydrocarbons. These pollutants have the potential for much greater effect on plants than CO.
public_commenters.note: Quote not found verbatim — flagged for HUMAN_REVIEW

public_commenters.name: Soil Conservation Service
public_commenters.quote: many soils maps lack complete information (symbols) and that soils descriptions and interpretations are not readily available to the reader

public_commenters.name: Department of Health Education and Welfare
public_commenters.quote: We feel there is a need for sanitary inspection both during the games and the off-season period. This provision should be included in the final EIS.
